title: The UK Today
overview: Modern Britain is a diverse and democratic nation of around 67 million people. Its four countries—England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland—share one government and monarch while each keeps its own identity and culture. The UK has a strong economy, free education, health care through the NHS, and a deep commitment to human rights and equality.

study_sections.heading: Basic Facts
study_sections.content: Population: about 67 million. Four countries: England, Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland. Capital cities: London, Edinburgh, Cardiff, Belfast. Official language: English (plus Welsh, Gaelic, Irish). Government: constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy. Currency: pound sterling (£).

study_sections.heading: Economy and Services
study_sections.content: Main industries: finance, technology, manufacturing, education, creative arts. NHS (1948) provides free health care. Education compulsory 5 to 18 years old. Service sector dominates economy.

study_sections.heading: Rights and Values
study_sections.content: Equality Act 2010 and Human Rights Act 1998 protect freedom and fairness. Diverse, multi-ethnic population. Promotes respect for all communities.

study_sections.heading: Global Role
study_sections.content: Member of Commonwealth of Nations and United Nations. Britain leads in science, medicine, film, music, and design. Public services include police, fire service, armed forces. BBC provides public broadcasting. Pinewood Studios produces films.

key_facts: The UK today is a diverse, democratic, and developed nation with global influence.
About 67 million people live across its four nations, reflecting varied ethnic, cultural, and linguistic backgrounds.
England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland share a union with devolved governments and distinct identities.
The UK is a constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy with the King as Head of State and the Prime Minister as Head of Government.
London is a leading financial and cultural centre, alongside major cities such as Birmingham, Manchester, Leeds, Glasgow, Cardiff, and Belfast.
The economy ranks among the world’s largest, powered by finance, technology, education, creative services, healthcare, and advanced manufacturing.
The City of London remains a major hub for international banking and commerce.
The UK belongs to organisations including the UN, NATO, Commonwealth, and WTO.
Following the 2016 referendum, the UK left the EU in 2020, reshaping trade and international relationships.
Institutions such as Oxford, Cambridge, and the Francis Crick Institute lead global science, medicine, and technology.
Environmental protection and renewable energy are national priorities.
Parliament tackles challenges in healthcare, education, social equality, and digital security.
Immigration contributes diversity, innovation, and global connections.
The Human Rights Act 1998 and Equality Act 2010 protect freedoms and equality before the law.
The NHS provides free healthcare, while education, the BBC, and volunteering support civic life.
British culture continues to influence the world through film, music, literature, fashion, and sport.
The UK faces issues such as regional inequality, climate change, and economic adjustment but remains committed to democracy and the rule of law.
Modern Britain’s identity rests on stability, diversity, and innovation working together.
memory_hook: See the UK today as a living mosaic—diverse communities, global industries, and trusted services like the NHS fitting together under shared democratic values.

options: England, Isle of Man, Scotland, Wales
